title: 2.4 and 2.5 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lessons 2.4 and 2.5!
topic_path: Early Humans (250,000 years BP to 3,000 BCE)
The Biggest Mistake Humans Ever Made? | 2.5

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best supports the idea that foraging communities may have lived better lives than early agriculturalists?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Fossil evidence of teeth and bones shows that foragers were generally healthier.
Written records describe complex art and frequent leisure activities.
Oral traditions suggest foragers were healthier and had more time for relaxation.
Geological evidence shows that foragers had a smaller impact on the environment.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following factors do historians believe contributed to the higher quality of life in foraging communities compared to early agricultural societies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Foragers had varied diets and spent less time working, leading to better health and more leisure.
Foragers lived in smaller groups that reduced the spread of disease and stress.
Foragers lived in large cities with advanced medical care and food storage systems.
Foragers used written language to organize labor and prevent overwork.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the development of food surpluses change the way early communities functioned?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It allowed for labor specialization and the growth of cities.
It made societies less centralized and more independent.
It forced communities to remain small and scattered.
It caused hierarchies to emerge, which then led to labor specialization.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the adoption of farming eventually contribute to the rise of slavery in human societies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Farming required large amounts of labor, which led some societies to force others to do the work through systems of enslavement.
Farming gave certain individuals more power, creating social hierarchies that made slavery possible.
Farming led to more advanced societies and trade networks, which eventually included the trade of enslaved people.
Farming created larger communities and increased warfare, which led to the capture of prisoners who could be enslaved.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following are major long-term drawbacks of agriculture that continue to affect societies today?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the depletion and destruction of natural systems
the rise of social hierarchies
limited technological and cultural advancements
weakened food production due to reliance on wild resources

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following characterizes a significant difference between early foraging and farming communities?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Foraging communities were nomadic, while farming communities established permanent settlements.
Foraging communities lived in permanent settlements, while farmers were nomadic.
Foraging communities had large populations, while farming communities were very small.
Foraging communities had formal governments, while farming communities remained egalitarian.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the shift from foraging to farming?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: People gradually began domesticating plants and animals, which over time led to permanent settlements.
Foragers quickly abandoned hunting and gathering once they learned about farming from other societies.
Communities intentionally developed farming after cities and governments were already established.
Farming was invented to replace trade between groups and eliminate the need to move around.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the transition from foraging to farming affect the size and structure of human communities?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Farming allowed people to settle in one place, leading to more densely populated communities.
Communities became smaller because farming limited access to food.
The shift to farming caused people to spread out more and avoid living in large groups.
The size of communities remained the same, but farming replaced hunting as the main activity.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the shift from foraging to farming change the structure and role of the family unit?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Families became larger and more settled, with clearly defined roles and responsibilities.
Families became less important as individuals began living and working alone.
Farming led to the end of extended families and made single-parent households more common.
The role of the family unit stayed the same, since foragers and farmers had similar lifestyles.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did the development of agriculture lead to an increase in infectious diseases?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Close contact with domesticated animals and dense human populations allowed diseases to spread more easily.
The repetitive physical labor from farming put stress on the body and weakened bones and muscles.
Cities built after the rise of farming attracted rats, which carried disease.
Trade networks expanded and helped spread diseases faster across regions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why is the rise of social hierarchies in early farming societies often considered a negative consequence of the transition from foraging?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Hierarchies concentrated wealth and power in the hands of a few, leading to increased social inequality.
Hierarchies allowed farming communities to abandon rules and laws in favor of individual freedom.
Hierarchies led to strict rules that prevented people from having any freedom or choice in their communities.
Hierarchies made people work harder than ever before without gaining any benefits or improvements in their lives.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best supports the claim that the transition to farming had an overall positive impact on human society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Farming enabled population growth and the development of permanent settlements.
Farming supported collective learning by allowing people to share knowledge and build on new ideas.
Farming simplified human diets by focusing on a few efficient staple crops that met all nutritional needs.
Farming made it possible for societies to stop relying on cooperation and focus solely on self-sufficiency.
